lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Well, good morning, everyone, and Happy New Year.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Welcome to the City of Mississauga's Budget Committee meeting for Monday, January the 12th.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: I think, as most of you know, we will be having Budget Committee meetings over the next couple of days, so I'm sure all my colleagues around the table are looking forward

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: to a spirited and informative discussion.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And, of course, I also want to thank the members of the public for participating in this process as well.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Seeing quorum, I'd like to call the meeting officially to order, and I'll begin by reading the Indigenous Lands Statement.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We acknowledge the lands which constitute the present-day city of Mississauga as being part of the treaty and traditional territory of the Mississaugas of the Credit First Nation, the Haudenosaunee Confederacy,

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: the Huron-Wendat and Wyandot Nations.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We recognize these peoples and their ancestors as peoples who inhabited these lands since time immemorial.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: The City of Mississauga is home to many global Indigenous peoples.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: As a municipality, the City of Mississauga is actively working towards reconciliation by confronting our past and our present, providing space for Indigenous peoples within their territory to recognize and uphold their treaty rights and to support

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Indigenous peoples.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And we formally recognize the Anishinaabe origins of our name and continue to make Mississauga a safe space for all Indigenous peoples.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: The agenda has been circulated.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Can I have a mover of the agenda?

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Councillor Ted Joe.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: All in favour?

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: That is carried.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Are there any declarations of conflict of interest?

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Seeing none, we'll move on to the minutes of the previous Budget Committee meeting.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: of October 1st, 2025.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Can I have a mover of those minutes?

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Councillor McFadden?

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Any discussion?

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: All in favour?

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Those are carried.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Thank you very much.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We have no presentations, but we do have a couple of deputations this morning.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Very pleased to welcome back our good friend Andrew Grantham, the Executive Director and Senior Economist of CIBC Capital Markets, regarding the economic

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: outlook arthur welcome back again thank you for being here and we look forward to your presentation thank you for for having me back all these years

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: That's okay.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: I've been called worse, don't worry.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So in terms of the economic outlook, I mean, I guess we're one down, unfortunately three to go when it comes to the Trump administration, his second administration here.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: We survived the first year, but we've got a lot to work through still in the coming year, particularly when it comes to the COSMA or the USMCA renegotiations.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Before I start talking about the Canadian economy and the local economy, the first thing we always have to do when we...

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: do any forecast for Canada is to set the benchmark about what we think US growth will be.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Because whether we like it or not, we are going to be very tied still to what's happening in the US.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And if the chaotic policies of this Trump administration slows down the US economy as well, that will slow down demand even above and beyond what tariffs themselves would do.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And we do expect that the policies of this Trump administration will slow

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: not just other countries but will slow the us as well we have immigration policy in the us now which is restraining labor supply and that's going to make it harder for the us economy to grow and the tariffs themselves are creating some inflationary pressures

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And that's making it harder for people to be able to buy as many goods as they otherwise would have done.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So compared to where we were, let's say, a year or just over a year ago before the tariffs were introduced, we expect the US over a two-year period to be about 1% weaker.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Now, that doesn't necessarily mean we're forecasting a recession of 1%.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: This is just 1% less growth than we would have done without these policies.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And tariffs and those policies are not the only reason to be a little bit cautious about the US at the moment.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: What we have also seen within the US economy

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: is that the drivers of growth within the U.S. are becoming much more concentrated.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So the U.S. economy has been growing this past year, in 2025.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: It was actually growing faster than a lot of people expected, given some of Trump's policies.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: But a lot of that was driven by the investments that we are making in AI.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And a lot of that was also driven by the fact that we had stock market gains which were

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: meaning that a lot of high income individuals had a lot of wealth gains and people were spending that money.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The sectors unrelated to that sort of investment or the spending of persons who are of lower income groups and who would not have had those wealth gains, that has been slower.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So we are seeing some underlying weakness in the US already.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: It's kind of been covered by one or two very, very specific and strong areas.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Now, in terms of where we are today in the Canadian economy, and there was a lot of ups and downs, there's a lot of revisions to our forecasts last year as tariffs were introduced and then exemptions were given, etc.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Where we are today, we are obviously in a worse place than we would have hoped, but in a better place than we feared, let's say, nine to 10 months ago.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Because what we have today is we have

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: very large tariffs, but only in a specific number of areas.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Those areas that are covered by what we call those sector-specific tariffs.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So steel, aluminum, autos, lumber, those sorts of areas we are paying very high tariffs on.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: But because we do have that COSMO USMCA exemption, these other goods that aren't covered by those sector-specific tariffs are paying very little in terms of their tariff rates.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: and where we are today in terms of the proportion covered or sorry not covered by those sector-specific tariffs around 80 to 85 percent of our trade with the us is still going there tariff free so this makes the upcoming cosmo usmca renegotiation extremely important because we have a lower average tariff rate than most other countries do with the us because we have this exemption now

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: In economics, we never promise anything, and certainly when it comes to dealing with Trump, I'm not going to promise anything.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: But we have heard over the last couple of months some slight positive signs that make us a little bit more positive that we can get this renegotiated and we can get this COSMA exemption continued.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And one of those things actually is the fact that we now

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: have been given a specific list of things that they want to negotiate on.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And the reason why we think that's a positive is you think back to when we had the fentanyl tariffs placed on us.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: There was not really much that Canada could do about those because that wasn't really a problem that we could solve because it wasn't a problem.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: They just wanted to put tariffs on us in the first place.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: We do have a list

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: now where we can negotiate that includes things like the dairy industry and things that have been kind of long-standing issues for the us so we do think it's not going to be a straight line but we do think that we will be able to get that trade deal renegotiated and that will mean that those sector-specific tariffs they may remain but our average tariff rate across the country

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: will remain quite low.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: That, unfortunately, is not the case locally for the Ontario economy because we do have a greater proportion of those industries that are paying sector-specific tariffs, so auto industry, steel, aluminum for Quebec there,

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: You know, Quebec and Ontario, we do have this average tariff rate, this hit to the economy, which is above the national average.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: It's really the other provinces that are benefiting the most from that COSMA exemption that we have.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So what have we been doing in terms of trying to deal with these US tariffs?

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Well, obviously we've been trying to negotiate, but that hasn't really got that far in terms of striking a trade deal as yet.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: But we are now making big investments to help us try and diversify our trade.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Now, this is exactly what we should be doing.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: This is going to be a positive for the Canadian economy.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: In terms of our economic forecasts or in terms of planning for the next year or two, the key question that we have to answer is, yes, it's gonna be a positive, but when?

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And in terms of the when, a lot of this spending or the greatest kind of impact of this spending will happen during the building phase and kind of during the peak building years.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: When will those be?

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Well, it's not gonna be year one.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: it's probably going to be years two and three so that's more kind of 2027 and 2028 so you know we do see this big fiscal spending try and diversify our economy spending on those infrastructure projects we probably should have been spending on 20 years ago that is a positive but it might not show up in the economic data straight away and particularly for the coming year

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So where is the Canadian economy right now?

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Unfortunately, when it comes to any economic indicators, particularly for Canada, where we do have more volatile data than some other countries, bigger countries,

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: There's no consistent message in terms of just how strong or otherwise the Canadian economy is, and particularly when it comes to the labor market.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: We have one survey of the labor market, the LFS there, that surveys people rather than employers.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And that suggests that employment growth over the last year has been very, very strong.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: We have another survey, which is the SEF survey, where they talk to employers, not employees, which suggests that employment growth has been very, very weak.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: What's the reality?

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Well, the reality probably lies somewhere in the middle.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: If we adjust the LFS data for population growth, we probably have seen job growth slightly higher than the employer survey, but certainly not anywhere near those headline levels.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And certainly something that has only really, on a national basis at least,

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: led to a fairly moderate decline in the unemployment rate.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The unemployment rate is still elevated on a national basis, but I think what we are seeing more locally, and this is one of the few indicators where I can talk locally about the Mississauga economy, what we are seeing locally here is actually better news because we saw a...

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So we actually saw a bigger increase in the unemployment rate in Mississauga during the period where the Canadian economy was struggling through the period of high interest rates.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: A lot of that was due to the fact that we had big population growth, a lot of newcomers.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Those are generally kind of weighted higher in Mississauga than in other areas anyway.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And that big population growth, we were struggling to find jobs for all of those people.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: I think what this chart shows here is that even though on a national basis the unemployment rate has come down only gradually from its peak last year, we've seen a greater improvement in Mississauga.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Yes, the unemployment rate is still above the national average, but we've seen a bigger improvement, a number of people actually being able to find jobs, being able to assimilate into the labor market now.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So what do we expect going forwards in terms of interest rates in order to try and speed up or otherwise this recovery?

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Well, unfortunately, what we are seeing in terms of interest rates today is a Bank of Canada that is much more reluctant than they were in the past to try and help the economy because they are haunted by what I call the ghost of inflation past.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: That was the 2022 inflation, that word transitory that they used,

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: They are now seeing that tariffs, the US action, are a supply shock that will create inflation.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And yes, we do need to stimulate the economy, but they want to be careful that they do not see that acceleration in inflation as well.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So where we are today, we are at an interest rate that is only just low enough

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: to really kind of accelerate growth within the economy.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And the Bank of Canada has pretty much told us that that's all that they are willing to do at the moment to try and help this recovery.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Because as I said, they are still worried about inflation.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And in our view,

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: too worried about inflation because even though we did see kind of in the first half of last year, we did see inflation accelerate on a national basis.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: A lot of that was driven by tariffs and the retaliatory tariffs that we put on the U.S.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Those retaliatory tariffs have now been more or less dropped.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And since that drop in, we've actually seen inflation decelerate again very close to their 2% target.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So in our belief, maybe the Bank of Canada should be doing a little bit more to stimulate the economy to help the recovery.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: But because they seem a little bit more cautious in today's environment, having gone through that 2022 inflationary period, our forecasts are based on the assumption that the Bank of Canada sets interest rates where they are currently, keeps them there for probably a year and a half.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: But at that level, what we are doing is we are accelerating the economy only fairly gradually.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: We are bringing the unemployment rate down only fairly gradually.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So this is going to be a recovery, but it's going to be kind of a prolonged recovery relative to what may have happened in the past when the Bank of Canada and other central banks were kind of really quick and forceful in terms of setting interest rates.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So the Ontario economy, because interest rates haven't come down maybe enough to improve the housing market, because that greater tariff risk that we have here

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: related to other provinces, probably going to stay below that national average in the coming year and only accelerate back above that in 2027, when hopefully, and I say hopefully, some of the risks around COSMA, the USMCA renegotiation, maybe some other sector-specific tariffs

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: can be reduced and we can actually accelerate this recovery that is underway within the economy.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: I'm going to leave it there, but obviously happy to take questions from everyone.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Thank you very much, Andrew.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: And you do have some questioners coming up.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: So questions to the deputant, Councillor Hornick.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Thank you so much, Mr.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Chair.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Thank you, Andrew.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Really appreciate you coming every year.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Very interesting, as always.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: When we always talk about the American economy, we've been talking a lot about a K-shaped economy right now where the very wealthy are doing very well and the not so well off are doing very poorly.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Do we see something like that in Canada or what would be the Canadian story for that breakdown of income earning?

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Yeah.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: It's not as obvious here in Canada, that K-shaped economy, because when you talk about that K-shaped economy, you're talking about the big investments in AI, the stock market performance that we've seen, those wealth gains.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: That's very evident in the US.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: It's not quite as evident here in Canada.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: There's more evidence that the whole economy is not doing particularly well, unfortunately.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: That said, we have had here as well,

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: you know, a good equity market performance over the last 12 months.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So lag in what the U.S. had seen before that, but we've seen that now.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So that could create those wealth gains, that could create a more K-shaped economy in 2026, but it's certainly not something we're seeing to the same extent as the U.S.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Okay, I always just ask, that's a good question to ask, just thinking about budget time and our taxation powers and where we should be targeting things.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So it's good to hear that

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: It's more even up here in Canada.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: You touched very briefly on housing.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And again, a lot of our budget in terms of maintaining infrastructure, building infrastructure for new housing is dependent here at the municipal level.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Can you talk more about the housing economy in Ontario and where you're kind of seeing that going in the future?

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Yeah, so I think this ties back to the Bank of Canada a little bit.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: We have a housing market at the moment where because the Bank of Canada is unwilling to cut interest rates too far because they're worried about inflation, we are going to have a housing market where demand is still fairly limited for a little while.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: On top of that, though, in Ontario, overall in the province, we still do have underbuilding relative to what we saw, you know, relative to kind of the population to units that we had in 2019.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So, you know, we've got quite a big level of underbuilding.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So we've got less supply.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: We've also got less demand.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So what do you need to kind of?

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: level that out and then see kind of demand outweigh supply.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: You probably need less of an improvement in demand because we still have that constrained supply.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: But when will that happen?

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: I doubt it's this year.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: It may be next year if we do get

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: you know, a little bit more clarity in terms of Cosmo, USMCA renegotiations.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The other thing I will say on housing, and I think, I guess maybe this ties in a bit to the K-shaped economy as well, a little bit, but

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: When it comes to the mortgage renewals that we've talked a lot about it in the past, just because interest rates are lower now doesn't mean that for some households that's not going to be a significant problem in this coming year.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And actually, when we look at the proportion of households that will see a large increase

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: positive mortgage renewal in terms of their payments will have to go up more than, let's say, 20%.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: That proportion is higher for this year than it was last year.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And so we still have a year going through where there's going to be, let's say,

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: It's only 5% of homeowners, maybe 10%, but that 5% to 10% will be struggling to afford the things that they were able to afford with that lower payment because a lot of them will see those big mortgage payments, and that will restrict the housing market activity as well.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Yeah, and I guess even on that, with interest rates being so much lower five years ago, many of the people who bought new houses were my age demographic, people who are family-oriented.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And so now that they've got much higher interest rates, you're seeing forced sales at a higher rate, right?

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: We could see more sales this year.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Yeah, I think, you know, what we typically say to people is that these won't be, because people talk about like these mortgage renewals causing higher sales.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: They think forced sales, right?

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: These aren't necessarily going to be forced sales because people were stress tested at higher interest rates.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: People relative to five years ago have seen quite good income growth.

lines.speaker: UNKNOWN
lines.text: So

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: It's not a case of not being able to afford the mortgage.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: It's being able to afford the lifestyle that they could with the lower mortgage payment.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So people could choose to move, that they want that lifestyle instead of that mortgage, but it's not necessarily going to be kind of forced movement.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Appreciate it.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Thank you very much.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Thank you very much, Councillor Hornet.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Councillor Mahoney.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Sorry, Madam Mayor, you want to be on?

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Okay, Madam Mayor.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Oh, I guess it's because you guys usually switch.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: your seats.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: That's probably why it is.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: You're Matt Mahoney today, Madam Mayor.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Congratulations.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Sorry, there's an issue with the AV system, so it has you labelled incorrectly.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: We are going to correct that a bunch.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Over to you, Madam Mayor.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: I've been called worse, I heard.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: It's an honour to be Matt Mahoney.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: I'd just like you to speculate a little bit, because we've put in tons of incentives for a building.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: We've got, I'm told, 23,284 units under

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: discussion right now with our staff.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: A lot of them have switched.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: The former condo builders have switched to rentals, which we love because we have a very low rental capacity in the city.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: My understanding with condos is people buy blocks of condos and they use it as an investment.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: Do you see that dying out completely?

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: It was a crazy phenomenon that started 25 years ago.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: are we going to be switching back to just straight rental buildings or what's going to happen with the condo industry?

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So I think what normally happens with anything in economics is you overshoot, then you undershoot, and then you come to a kind of sustainable level, right?

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So I think the proportion...

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: of condos versus rentals before the pandemic and kind of during the pandemic was unsustainable.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: At the rental rates that people were buying the condos for they were really kind of banking on the condo price going up to make money.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: which obviously that can't happen forever.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: But where we are today, because the economics of it, the rent price versus the condo price really doesn't work.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: We've seen that proportion fall to pretty close to zero, right?

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And it's now all kind of purpose-built rental.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: I think for a healthy market, we need some of both, but we need more purpose-built rental than we were seeing before the pandemic and more condo building than we do now.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: In terms of what will make the condo building and those sales...

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: increase again as a proportion, I think it comes down to the affordability of those units.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And what we will need to see is probably another year or two of very little price growth in those units.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So some income growth so people can afford those.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Maybe slightly lower interest rates than they are today.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And that's what's going to rebalance that market.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: For the next year or two, I think that purpose-built rental is still going to be the biggest and a much higher contributor than what we will see longer term.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: So if we were going to ask the federal government to do something more spectacular than what they're doing right now, which is practically nothing, we would be pushing incentives for rentals.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Yeah, I think we still have the overhang in the pandemic of underbuilding relative to the population, and the current economics, particularly in Ontario, makes it a lot easier to fill that through the purpose-built rental units than anything else right now.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: It's not necessarily the case across the country, but for Ontario, that's certainly the case, yeah.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: Good, thank you very much.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Okay, members of committee, I don't see any other names on the board.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Councillor Hornick, would you like to move receipt of the deputation?

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: All in favour?

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: That is carried.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Andrew, again, thank you very much for coming.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: And hopefully we'll see you next year, assuming we're all here next year.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Hopefully things will be a bit calmer by next year, maybe.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Anyway, thank you very much, as always.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Our next deputation is our own Christina Kakaflikas.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: who is our Director of Economic Development, as she's presenting regarding Mississauga's economic update.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Christina, welcome.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Good morning, Mr.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Chair, Mayor Parrish, and members of committee.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: I'm pleased to present Mississauga's economic update this morning, which provides a focused look at local economic conditions following Andrew's presentation.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: We'll review local economic indicators, business investment trends, and our business outlook.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: This slide focuses on a number of businesses or firms' growth compared to last year, and you'll see that Mississauga is tracking closely with the Ontario average.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: We're seeing consistent and incremental growth

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: growth in line with provincial and regional numbers.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And overall, these trends point to a stable and diversified economic base.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So this slide focuses more on the employment picture.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Labor market conditions as illustrated specifically by unemployment rate as well as job postings.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: As you saw previously in Andrew's presentation, the unemployment rate has been steadily increasing through 2023 and 2024 and it peaked in the middle of 2025 before easing in the last quarter.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So while the current rate remains elevated, it is comparable to the rest of the Toronto region as well as just slightly above the provincial average.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Hiring demand has stabilized, as you can see by the blue bars, following a post-pandemic surge in 2023.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Taken together, these indicators suggest a labor market that's stabilizing, but it's still adjusting to current market conditions and monetary policy and global macroeconomic conditions.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: With that broader economic context in mind, let's now look at business investment and development activity.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So non-residential building activity, sort of the theme word for the slide is a rebalancing.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: You can see that after several strong years of industrial development, it's pulled back a little bit, it's eased slightly, and you see more of an increase in retail and office activity.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Notably, the majority of office and retail activity has been in reinvesting in existing inventory rather than new supply.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: For industrial activity, it's sort of half and half, new versus existing reinvestment to existing inventory.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Commercial and industrial business permits have been spread across the city, across sectors, and it's not one single project that's driven this.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: It's really a number of investments across all activity.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So this suggests that businesses are continuing to invest incrementally, responding to market conditions and their operational needs rather than making big speculative investments.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So I'll ask you, it's a lot on this slide, but I'll ask you just to focus your attention on the left side of the screen.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So we see in the last year 65 new investments or expansion projects have landed in Mississauga, representing about 5,100 jobs and $2.2 billion of capital invested.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: On the right side of the screen, you can see the source of some of these investments, and it's interesting to note that a large portion, the majority in fact, of the investment has been driven by companies in Canada, Canadian companies, including Mississauga companies.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: That said, we still see strong investment from international markets, including the United States, and markets within Europe and Asia.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Compared to provincial trends, investment into Mississauga remained stable even as provincial investment levels cooled a little.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Together these patterns point to a strong economy supported by both local reinvestment as well as global engagement.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So this slide shows the industries that made up the investments in 2025, and the activity was also notably diverse.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Manufacturing accounted for the largest share alongside smart logistics, life sciences, and professional services and technology.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: This diversity is important.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: It helps insulate the local economy from sector shocks, and it helps build a more resilient economy.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The alignment between the investment activity as well as our economic developments strategy to support key growth sectors also is reassuring and reinforces the relevance of our sector growth strategies.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So these two slides, this one and the next one, really focus on some examples of company investments that landed in Mississauga.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: You'll recall that in late 2024, AstraZeneca announced their major investment, which is currently unfolding.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: They're making improvements to the space, and they'll be occupying that space later this year.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: also manufacturing investors, facility expansion at Lely Holdings, and Cerulea is a retention file.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: They've re-signed their lease and deciding to stay in Mississauga.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: You see investments, new investments from Schneider Electric and Albasol and Apex Logistics.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So these are examples of reinvestments, retention, and new investments.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: On this slide, you'll see investments from our startup community.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So beyond large capital investments, like the ones I pointed to in the previous slide, these are scaling companies that we've supported through our IDEA Step Up program.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And they're really great examples of innovation in Mississauga.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Our mandate in economic development is to support the larger companies, but also through these programs, support the startups and scale-ups.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: X-Atoms, as an example, is a clean tech company, supported through IDEA's step-up program.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: The company has secured early-stage financing, and it's advancing innovative water purification technology.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So that's really an exciting company, along with HDAC's therapeutic and food, Mamba.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: By supporting high-potential firms as they move forward with commercialization, Mississauga's building a pipeline of future employers and technology leaders that can scale locally over time.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Business sentiment provides insights onto how firms are experiencing current economic conditions.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: The next couple of slides speak to this business outlook.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: You can see here the top business obstacles that companies have identified, and this is a Statistics Canada survey that's done quarterly, are cost-related obstacles.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Things like real estate,

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Insurance, inflation, and input costs are really weighing on these companies.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Businesses also point to demand and market conditions as a challenge, and that includes increased competition and shifting consumer demands.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And lastly, labor-related obstacles.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So that could be finding the right talent and retaining talent.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Businesses are being cautious overall.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We can see from the data reinforcing the need to foster locally a competitive economy, one with the right types of supports to assist our companies.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So looking ahead,

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: What are businesses expecting in the next year or planning in the next year?

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Most of them, 55%, are not planning any major changes.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: 26% are planning on expansions, 20% on restructuring, and 3% are planning to close.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Expansion plans vary by sector.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: I'd say that most of those expansion plans would be modest and they're on the larger side of the companies.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: The companies that are looking to restructure are likely medium-sized companies across different sectors and in response to current economic conditions.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And so overall, it's more of an adjustment period rather than a period of decline or acceleration.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So these conditions set the stage for how the city can support businesses and position the economy moving forward.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Mississauga's ongoing resilience is really driven by its economic diversity.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So we're going to continue on that path.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: While the pace of growth has moderated from previous years, the city is continuing to attract private sector-led investment across diverse sectors.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And we're supporting overall employment stability and adjusting to changing economic conditions.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Importantly, the city and other governments and agencies are investing in infrastructure projects that are playing a role in sustaining our economic development.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Major infrastructure projects like the Here Ontario LRT, the Trillium Hospital, the airport expansion, those are all important in continuing to create jobs and contributing to the local economy.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Looking ahead, our updated strategy will drive our priorities through 2030 and will be presented to Planning and Development Committee on January 26.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And our strategy will integrate creative industries as well as retail and placemaking.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: These sectors contribute not only to economic activity, but they also contribute to vitality, sense of place, vibrancy, and identity and branding.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And aligning these efforts with the broader economic development strategy will ensure that growth is both competitive and inclusive.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Thank you very much, Christina.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Questions of the deputant, Mayor Parrish.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: It's actually me we're going to keep you on your toes.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Oh, okay, sorry.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: I thought they'd fix that upstairs.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The mayor and I are switching today.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Deputy Mayor Mahoney.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Thank you very much, Mr.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Chair, and thank you, Christina.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Just really a quick question and maybe just a topic of conversation out of curiosity.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: It's all things tariffs, and we talk about some of the concerns and challenges with the economy with the U.S. market.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Are we seeing in Mississauga or the GTA any opportunities for companies who perhaps would look at expanding to the U.S. that are perhaps looking at markets in Canada or in the GTA, just for more certainty and a bit of a gateway into North America and having more

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Certainty in our economy are we seeing any of those trends or is it something that we're benchmarking?

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Through you mr.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Chair, thank you for the question if I understood correctly is you're asking if we're seeing more companies looking at the Canadian economy as a option given the uncertainty and Changing trade policies in the US.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Yes.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Okay.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Great.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: UNKNOWN
lines.text: I

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We are.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We're actually, my team in the business investment and sector development unit is fielding multiple inquiries and we're obviously very pleased to see that.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We're working with a number of companies, Europe and Asia based, and so the year

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: has started off pretty strong, more than we've expected.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Now, whether that continues and to what degree it continues, we will see, but certainly we're encouraged by the volume of inquiries that we're working on right now.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Okay, yeah, thanks.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And really just a general question, Christina, because I do know that in some of the conversations, obviously there are concerns with the uncertainty in the U.S. market and

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: putting your investment dollars in the United States, perhaps it's an opportunity for us here, not just in Mississauga, but the GTA.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: I mean, obviously, Mississauga is the second largest economy in the province and growing.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So any opportunities that we can explore or continue to look at that can bring businesses here that might have gone south of the border, I think would be positive.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: But really, just a curiosity point if there's opportunities, and it seems like there are.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So thank you so much to you, and thank you to the team for all the work you do.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Thank you, Councillor Mahoney.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Councillor Hart.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Thank you, Chair, and through you.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Following up on the Deputy Mayor's question, I just want to expand a little bit on that, if you would, in terms of how do we track and how are we handling the responsiveness to inquiries that are coming, particularly from outside of the Canadian market?

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Because I know finding us can be like the first step, if you will, in us providing the solution for them to be expanding their business.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: And so I know we do a lot of work in terms of marketing our city on the tourism side, on other issues, but how do we feel we're stacking up against that criteria from attracting business perspective?

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Through you, Mr.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Chair, thank you for the question.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Our team in business investment and sector development works very closely with partners across the globe, trade commissioner offices, Ontario international investment offices in key markets.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And so that group, that segment, is typically our largest pipeline for investment inquiries.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So they're embedded in market.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: They...

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: meet with companies and potential investors on a regular basis.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: A lot of our visibility, branding, promotion is very targeted.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: It's less about sort of a gunshot approach, pardon the term, and more of a very focused marketing effort, both through general sort of

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Generally understood marketing approaches, campaigns, brand identity kind of campaigns, but it's also about relationship building and making sure that we're in front of those people who are actually fielding requests in market.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: letting them know about what we're focused on, where the growth is, giving them examples of companies that invested, communicating our value proposition in a clear manner.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So we're very targeted in that sense.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We also work with existing companies.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: You can see by the investment chart that

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: It's so important to make sure that you're working your existing companies, and I know you're an expert at that yourself, having worked in the private sector as well, and so really working closely with existing companies to help them with their expansion plans, and that's a lot of the work that our team does.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: In terms of how we track all of that,

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We have a CRM.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We are a very formal process in terms of tracking all the engagements with all of our clients so that we know how long it's been since we last interacted with them, what their questions are, how we can support them, where their pain points are, and we do consistent follow-up to make sure that we're in front of them and consistently working to support them in their investment decisions.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Fantastic.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Thank you so much for that detailed answer.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Thank you, Councillor Hart and Mayor Parrish.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: Yes, thank you.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: You talk about creative industries in retail and placemaking.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: We're about, the staff's worked very hard on bringing forward a plan for city centre that looks at a conference centre and looks at hotels.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: Can you expand, not just on that, but on the creative industries?

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: The more, I know Councillor Das goes very much into arts and theatre, so am I.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: and we are trying to expand that.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: I know Toronto makes a lot of money from creative industries, and we so far are just dipping our toes in it.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Through you, Mr.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Chair, Mayor Parrish, thank you for the comment and the question.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We agree.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: I'm really excited that Creative Industry joined the economic development team, Invest Mississauga, last year.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We are working with that team.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Firstly, I just want to point out that next year's presentation will incorporate more data points.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We're looking at how we track that economic impact of creative industries.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We have some numbers, but we don't have sort of that time series data.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So we're going to be digging into that so we can make sure we're tracking progress.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: But just from a...

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: sort of the question you're asking about the importance and significance of that sector, it's not only about the direct economic impact from the way I see it.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Of course, that is very important in terms of jobs, in terms of investment.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: It's also sort of the halo effect of creative industries and how a city is

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: perceived by both investors, visitors.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: It's sometimes the front door to a municipality when somebody can experience arts and culture.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: It really adds to the sense of place of a city and speaks to the creative talent of a city.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And so those kinds of investments are really important from our perspective.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: It also helps to draw talent.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So any company that's looking for talent of any age and skill level

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: to be able to point to creative experience, to be able to enhance the brand of a city from an arts and culture perspective, from a creative industries perspective, is a strength, and it is something that helps us to attract both talent and visitors, as well as people who are there to consume

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: the creative experience.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And so for me, it's a really exciting point in economic development to welcome creative industries, to measure and to grow it as a sector and its economic impact, but also to help strengthen the city as an urban place, you know, that's changing, that's exciting, that's vibrant.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: Okay, as long as we make money doing it, I'm very interested.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: If I could, Mr.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: Chair, I did want to speak to this a little bit as well because

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: As we dig into this, the professional creative industries in Mississauga have not really been financially supported to the extent that they should be.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: We've really focused primarily on the amateur side of the arts through festivals and events and other things.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: What we will be doing this year is really digging into that because I do feel there's a lot of opportunity for that to happen.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: And you're right, other cities put far more money into that sector.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: Our music strategy, as a good example, is really, from my estimation, terribly underfunded.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: So there is opportunity.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: We need to look at the portion of the city's mat tax to really bolster that part of the industry.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: So I'll be working with Christina and the team this year to really flesh that out and bring back a more realistic budget, I think, for what we expect to realize through these strategies.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: If I may continue, Studio Bottega is a perfect example.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: They came in with a complex where they had big studios ready to build and all the stuff that feeds into it,

lines.text: building sets and doing all that and it's languishing.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And it's really a shame because I remember when we first saw it was way back four or five years ago.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Everybody was pretty excited about it.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: But they just can't do it on their own.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Okay, thank you very much.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Thank you very much, Madam Mayor.

lines.text: Councillor Kovac.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: Very sorry through you, Mr.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: Chair.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: I wanted to ask Christina, one of the slides, 13, I could have taken this offline, but

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: being that it was in, how accurate of a formulation is this?

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: What to expect from businesses within the next year if we have about 100,000?

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: I don't know, Mississauga Board of Trade must represent about 1,000.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: Were they part of helping to inform any of this?

lines.text: Can you just maybe speak very, very briefly to how accurate you feel this is, this projection?
