lines.text: Through you, Mr.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Chair, thank you for the question.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: That sentiment or business outlook is taken from a national survey of businesses done by Statistics Canada.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And so the survey is done every quarter to measure business sentiment.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So it's not a local survey that we led, but we do monitor that survey and it helps us as one of the inputs in terms of

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: anticipating how the businesses are feeling and where they're focusing on and what to anticipate in the coming quarter.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We feel it's the best indicator that we have access to, but it gives us a national picture rather than a very local picture.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: I should say, though, that we have delved deeper into the survey itself, and the numbers that I've shown are Mississauga-focused.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: They're reflective of the city, but it's through a national survey.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: That's appreciated.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Thank you very much, Councillor Kovac.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: I don't think I see anyone else on the board.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Councillor Kovac, you'll move receipt of the deputation.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Thank you very much.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: All in favour?

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: That is carried.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Thank you very much, Christina, and to your team.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Our next item is 7.3, related to item 10.2 on the agenda.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: We have Robert Trewartha, Director, Strategic Communications and Initiatives, and Sarah Lamb, the Senior Research Director from Forum Research Inc., joining us today.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: I believe the vendor that is responsible for the survey is also available

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: if there are questions.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: But Rob, we'll turn it over to you and Sarah, and the floor is yours.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: Thank you very much, Mr.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: Chair, and good morning, Chair and members of committee.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: I'm joined today by Sarah Lamb, the Senior Research Director at Forum Research, who is our vendor.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: to discuss the results of the 2025 Resident Engagement Survey.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: We undertake this survey every two years to give us a baseline on how residents experience our services and their level of satisfaction with the city.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: We decided to do it this year a little bit closer to the budget to help inform your deliberations.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: Sarah will provide greater context on the data and then we'll be here to answer any questions that you may have afterwards.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: So Sarah, over to you.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So just to start off with a bit of an introduction before I get right into the results of the survey, a bit of background of Forum Research.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: We are a Canadian research firm with over 30 years of experience.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And over these years, we've supported many municipalities across Canada in similar research services, understanding resident perspectives and sentiments.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So like Ryan mentioned, today I'll be going over just the overall results from this 2025 survey.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Following this presentation afterwards, there will be the full reports available online.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Throughout this presentation, you'll also see comparisons to previous citizen satisfaction surveys that were done before.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: I do want to note that some of these comparisons were used with a different methodology.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So this year's survey was through an online methodology, and in previous years they were done either phone, telephone, or online.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Now, for this year's survey, it was fielded last year, between October 6 to November 6.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: All residents of the city of Mississauga who are at least 18 years of age or older were qualified to participate in this survey.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: However, we did exclude city employees from participation.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: In total, we received 1,002 completed survey responses, and the average length of the survey was almost 14 minutes long.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: I do want to mention that all of the respondents were drawn from an online panel of Mississauga residents.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And we did weight the data afterwards to ensure that it is representative of the population of Mississauga.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And this is based on the most recently available Statistics Canada census data from 2021.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Now diving right into the results of the survey, I'm gonna start with satisfaction levels.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So we do see that satisfaction levels among residents are pretty comparable compared to the previous years.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: This year we see that 76% of residents are satisfied with the services provided by the city, and 18% provided neutral rating.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And then when it comes to the city's municipal government, 66% are satisfied, while 22% are neutral.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Within the survey, we also asked residents to name what's the most important issue that they think is facing Mississauga today.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And the most popular option was either

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: housing or rental costs and availability, with 17% citing this, and also 18% citing crime and safety concerns.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: We also asked residents what's something that they think will improve the city of Mississauga for a better future.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And 17% mentioned housing or rental costs and availability.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: When it comes to value for taxes, we see that 49% of residents rated the value of tax dollars as

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: a very good value or a good value while 25% remained neutral.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And then we also asked residents their opinions and perspectives in terms of balancing programs and service levels with taxation and user fees.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So here we asked residents to pick their preference among three options.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: First one is increasing program levels and services, which also means, of course, an increase in taxation or user fees.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Second option is maintaining current programs and service levels, which also means increasing taxes and user fees.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And then the third option is keeping taxes and user fees the same, which means a cut to programs and service levels.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So we see here that 59% do agree with increasing taxes or user fees.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: with 34% of them more so wanting to maintain existing programs and services, and 25% wanting to add new ones.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And so when we break this down even further, looking at those 59% who agree to increasing taxes and user fees, we see that most of them would prefer this be done through user fees, so 47%, compared to 19% preferring this be done through increases in property taxes.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Within the survey, we also asked about budget priorities.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So on the left-hand side here, we asked residents to pick what they think the city of Mississauga should prioritize in terms of their budget.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So here we see that 59% suggested prioritizing spending on affordable property taxes and fees, 54% on infrastructure maintenance, and 45% on investments and essential services.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: We also asked them about their thoughts on the cost-sharing formula for regional services with other Peel municipalities.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And we did see that 81% believe that the cost-sharing formula should be changed to be based on population size.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And then lastly, when we asked how familiar they are with the city's budget, 36% said that they're familiar, while 25% remained neutral.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And here is a quick summary of resident satisfaction levels when it comes to all the various services offered by the city of Mississauga.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So I'm just going to quickly go through them.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: When it comes to fire and emergency services, we see a satisfaction rating of 81%.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: MyWay Transit Services sees a satisfaction rating of 69%.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Library Services at 82%, which is actually the highest satisfaction rating compared to all the other services.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Arts and culture and heritage services at 72%, recreation services at 74%, and parks and forestry services at 79%.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And then when it comes to the city's overall regulatory services, 62% are satisfied with that.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: When it comes to housing affordability, we did see that 72% believe that house and rent prices are likely to

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: either moderately or significantly impact their ability to live in the city over the next five years.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Within the survey, we also asked about flood prevention, so we see that 52% were actually at different levels of being impacted by the two severe flooding events since July 2024.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And then of those who were impacted by the flooding events, about half, so 49%, rated the city's management efforts in these situations as good or excellent.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And then more than half were also satisfied with the city's various flood prevention and support efforts.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: When it comes to transportation and mobility services, 60% are satisfied with the overall quality and condition of the roads in the city.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And when we asked about various services that the city does in order to make sure that the roads are well maintained and efficient and safe, we do see that satisfaction ratings range from 51% to 68%.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And overall, 70% of residents believe that the city's road safety measures

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: is helping in achieving Vision Zero.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And then when it comes to environmental action, 64% rate the average amounts of litter in public spaces managed by the city as none, very little, or just some.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And then lastly, 66% think that climate change does have a moderate to large impact on Mississauga.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: This slide talks about communication preferences, so just half are satisfied with how well the city communicates about its services, while 28% remain neutral.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: 54% agree that the city offers meaningful opportunities for residents for engagement and consultation.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: and just about a third, 37%, have participated in at least one of these public consultations or engagement processes in the last year.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And of those who did, 69% are satisfied with the city's public consultation and engagement processes.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: We also found that in the last year, just about a quarter, 23%, have contacted the city, and most of them contact the city using the 311 or direct city number.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And most of them, 69%, are satisfied with the overall customer service that's been provided to them.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Here I'll quickly wrap up my presentation today with this little neat little chart here.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: It basically shows the impact of each individual services on residents overall satisfaction levels and so on the horizontal axis here you'll see that importance range and then on the vertical axis we'll see

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: the performance levels of each of these services, so how well they are performing in terms of satisfaction.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So we see on the right-hand side at the bottom,

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: regulatory services, that has a high importance on overall satisfaction, but it is performing lower compared to other services, so this is a primary area for improving satisfaction.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And then on the opposite top right, fire and emergency services, this is also important, but performance is a lot higher, satisfaction is higher, and so it's a primary area for maintaining satisfaction.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: That brings me to the end.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Like I mentioned, the full reports will be made available online after today's presentations.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Did you have anything to conclude with, Rob?

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: No, you're good?

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Okay, thank you very much for that presentation.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: You know, I look at these survey results and it always reminds me of a famous line of a former city manager who was here for many years who said, nobody wants to die, but everybody wants to go to heaven.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: And even though I think the results of the survey were excellent in many regards, and I think most residents of Mississauga think they get very good service by our city at a reasonable expenditure, it's always interesting to see those stats.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Just before I take questions from members of the committee, 10.2 we'll deal with on the consent agenda, as there may be people in public question period that want to address it.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: So we'll just go to questions of the deputants.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: We'll start with Councillor Reid.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Thank you, Mr.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Chair.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Thank you so much for your presentation.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: And I was wondering if you could just speak to the sample size, because that's always a question of concern.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: A thousand people, so you're doing this presentation and it's not, it's the respondents, it's not the residents, it's like such a small portion.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: At the end of the survey, I saw that there was a link to look at past satisfaction surveys.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Can you just speak to how many, what has been the most number of people to do a sample size for this over the past years?

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: what this means with just having a thousand and two people to represent the city on this your survey

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Okay.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Through you, Mr.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Chair, that is an excellent question, actually, and I get that question a lot.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: I know that the sample size of 1,002 in comparison to the whole population of Mississauga, it seems like really little.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: However, statistically speaking, it is a very large sample size to work with.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So basically how it works is...

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: You know, this is an online survey, so it is representative of the population.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: However, it's not fully a probability sample, this kind of getting into the nitty-gritty of it.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: However, if it is a true probability sample, if it is, we can use something called a margin of error to better understand how

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: you know, accurate our results are.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And so I don't know if we're able to go back to, I think it was the third slide of the presentation.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Maybe not.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: But I believe for this study with a sample size of 1,000, it gives us a margin of error of, I want to say about like 3% to 5%.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So that means if it is a true probability sample, all of our results here, it'll fall within that plus or minus 3.1%.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And the thing with margin of error is that, yes, if you increase your sample size, it reduces that margin of error because you're getting closer to, you know, your total population.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: However, it doesn't increase at the same rate.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So at some points, even if you're increasing our sample size to like 2,000, it doesn't really improve the margin.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Exactly.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So I would say basically the sample size of 1,002 is a very strong sampling.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: And does that factor into demographics?

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Yes, of course, yes.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So at the end of data collection, we do weight the results.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So all of the results that you saw here today are weighted by age, gender, and ward to reflect the population of Mississauga.

lines.speaker: Councillor Brad Butt
lines.text: Great, thank you.

lines.speaker: Councillor Brad Butt
lines.text: Thank you very much, Councillor Reid.

lines.speaker: Councillor Brad Butt
lines.text: Madam Mayor.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Yeah, I have a question to staff.

lines.text: How many people actually use the transit system in Mississauga?

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: What percentage?

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: MR.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Through you, Mr.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Chair, I know the annual ridership is in the 50 million range.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: I'll have to get that number in terms of percent of unique riders, but the average annual ridership is around 50 million passengers.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: MS.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: But if you could estimate, of 700,000 people, how many are consistent transit riders?

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: I think three, Mr.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Chair.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: We'll take that offline, and we'll just try and get that number.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: I'm building a question.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: I'm wondering how many underrepresented sectors of our population would not do an online survey.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: You're going to have a lot of people that don't speak English as their first language.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: I don't know how you went about finding your 1,000, so I'm wondering if there are any particular groups that wouldn't have an opportunity to express themselves on this.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Through you, Mr.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Chair, that is a really good question.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Like I said, there are some challenges and implications with online survey research like this.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And sometimes it does have a bias towards certain demographic groups or populations.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: However, there has been past research

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: you know, a lot of research is being done through online panels.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And there's been past research with online panels, even with election polling.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And we do see that it is very comparable to more traditional methods like the telephone method.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And so...

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: There are challenges with the online methodology, but it is still fairly representative of the population, and it is a good methodology, especially considering, you know, the length of the survey and budget purposes.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Well, the example I would use, when you say 69% of my way riders responded a certain way, how do you know they actually ever stepped onto a bus?

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Well, that's the thing with not just online surveys, with any surveys at all.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: You're trusting that the respondent is providing an answer that reflects their own views.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And obviously there are some that might not, but generally people do answer based on their own opinions.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Well, the giveaway is when I read through the survey, it showed that something like

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Almost 80% of people prefer to be communicated with by email or text.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Now, that just doesn't work with 700,000 people in a city.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: So you're hitting people that are prone to using email when you're doing this survey.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: You're not hitting all the people that don't use that method of communication.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: The underrepresentation worries me.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: And when you get a group as high as you did that prefer high taxes, that doesn't match any of the phone calls we get in our offices, not one bit.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: So that thing's way skewed.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: And they obviously understood the cost-sharing difficulty we've got with Brampton on the police because that showed up really high at 81%.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: So our communication with them is working there.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: But I'm not thrilled with online communication

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: I don't think it's very representative of the people we have here in Mississauga, particularly when, as I say, that one number I looked at is really skewed.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Everybody that took your survey preferred to be emailed or texted.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: They don't want to see our newsletters.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: They don't want to see anything else.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: So I just, I think that the era for doing these is over.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: I think we need to be doing focus groups.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: I think we have to be paying more attention to a good mix of citizens.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: We also have to understand if you're going to comment on transit, you have to be a transit rider.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: I don't want to hear what the guy following the bus thinks in his car because the bus is slowing him down.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: So I'm less than thrilled with this than I would like to be.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.text: Councillor Hart.

lines.text: And through you, first question is sort of a technical question.

lines.text: because i think it's hard when we're doing comparisons if we're not consistent right um and so i think we you know to the mayor's point if we're going to look at how we're doing this or what we're doing with this we have to sort of build in some guardrails to make sure that we are asking questions that we want to continue to track right i would suggest that my way would definitely be one of those from a budget perspective but i look forward to seeing the full report with the waiting and the other data as a polling data geek

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Good morning, Madam Mayor, members of the Budget Committee.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: It's a privilege for me to be here, present the city's budget, 2026 proposed budget for the city of Mississauga.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Before I begin, I would like to take this opportunity to recognize the amazing work that my finance team has done, particularly our budget and business planning team, the various financial strategies team and the business services team.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Their dedication and expertise have been instrumental in bringing the budget forward.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: I would also like to acknowledge the tremendous collaboration that we received from the service areas and particularly roads, transit, parks and recreation teams.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: This slide, let's get into the budget details now.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So this slide outlines the framework guiding the preparation and presentation of the 2026 budget under the strong mayor power framework and legislation.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Under the legislation, the mayor is required to present a proposed budget no later than February 1st of the budget year.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The legislation allows the mayor to delegate the responsibility for preparing the budget to staff.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: For 2026, Mayor Parrish has exercised his option and directed staff to prepare a proposed and recommended budget.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The proposed 2026 to 2029 business plan and 2026 budget document was published last week, January 6th, which is well ahead of the February 1st deadline.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: This early release has provided Council and the public with ample time to review and engage before the deliberations begin today.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: This slide outlines the key steps and timelines for approving the 2026 budget under the Strong Mayor Power Framework.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The 2026 budget starts today.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Budget committee meeting will continue tomorrow.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And we do have two backup dates on the 19th and the 20th.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: On 28th of January, council will meet to approve any budget amendments, if there are any.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: But approval is required for council if they wish to shorten the standard 30-day amendment time period.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Item 10.4 is related to that particular shortening and which will be brought forward at the appropriate time for approval.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Following the amendment period, the mayor has 10 days to exercise a veto on any of the amendments if required.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: If a veto occurs, council has 15 days to override it with a two-third majority vote.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The budget is deemed adopted at the end of the period, amendment veto or override, unless shortened by the council and the mayor.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Let's talk about the business planning priorities.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So the refreshed corporate priorities have been instrumental in shaping the 2026 to 2029 business plan and the 2026 budget.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: These updates ensure stronger alignment with emerging needs and evolving strategic objectives.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The updated priorities are listed on the slide.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Advancing on the city's strategic vision, deliver the core services, tackle housing affordability with Mayor's Housing Task Force, move forward with city building, and implement cost containment strategies.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Budget education, as part of the annual budget cycle, the city runs an extensive budget education campaign to ensure the residents and businesses understand budget and how they can get involved.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: These are the various campaigns used this year listed on the slide.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: In particular, I just wanted to talk about many councillors hosted work-specific town halls, which were very well received by the residents.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: This slide talks about the high level of how the city funds its operations and where those dollars are allocated.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: On the revenue side, property tax is the biggest component of where the funds are generated, which is around 56%, followed by user fees, around 17%, and then some provincial funding at around 12%.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: On the expense side, the biggest expense is still labor, 54%, followed by transfer to the reserves of around 19%.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: These transfers are really important because to support our capital program and to maintain our financial health.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Then we have contractor and professional services at around 7%.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: This is an important slide because it tells the city only receives 37% of the property tax, but it delivers more than 200 services, such as transit, parts maintenance, recreational libraries, and emergency services, et cetera.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The region collects 48% of the property taxes and 15% is collected by the province for education.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: On the commercial side, the city only retains 26%.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: 39% goes to the province and 35% is retained by the region.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Let's talk a little bit about the roads transition.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Peel Transition Implementation Act 2025 received royal assent on December 11, 2025.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: This means that the city will be responsible for all the roads within its boundaries.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Transferring these services to Mississauga will result in savings for taxpayers, but detailed financial information is required before these changes can be incorporated in the city budget.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: As such, the regional orders have not been included in the city's proposed 2026 to 2029 business plan or budget.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Original transfer date is July 1st, 2026.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: In December 2025, the province is proposing a regulation to change the effective date to July 1st, 2027.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Staff will be providing an update on the financial impacts of the regional roads transfer at a later date, including any budget savings for 2026 and future years.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Now let's talk about a little bit more on the 2026 budget.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The 2026 budget has been very challenging to assemble because of the non-discretionary pressures the city's facing, particularly union labor increases.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The 2026 budget had been forecasted to increase by 13.3% during the 2025 budget cycle.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Amid these challenges, circumstances, city staff were tasked to find ways to reduce the budget using tactics like deferring taxes, cutting discretionary budgets and increasing revenue.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Staff were able to identify 17.4 million efficiencies or a reduction of 2.3% in budget.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The other options available to reduce the overall budget was to reduce our levies.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: We are proposing to reduce the capital levy from 3% to 1% and pausing on the public safety fire program for the 2026 year.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Additionally, transferring from reserves have been done or proposed to be done to reduce the tax impact.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: This slide talks about how we assemble the budget.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Just to break it down to different components, we start with maintain current service levels.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: We reduce efficiencies, reduce assessment growths, add new initiatives, and then add the levies.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: I'll go into each details of each of the components.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The first item, developing operating budget each year, is the cost to maintain current service levels.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: These costs include things like labor, materials, software licenses, and are offset by revenue increases.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The cost to maintain current service levels in 2026 contributes around 84.5 million, which is 11.31% of the city's net operating budget.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: If you notice that the majority of the component here is labor, so I would like to elaborate a little bit more on the labor side, which is on the next slide.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: as you see that 76% of the maintained current service level is labor, and out of that,

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: at 76%, 68% is due to union settlements.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So this is the biggest driver or the biggest news item for 2026 budget.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: As part of our commitment to cost containment, the city reviews the budget line by line to identify savings.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: For 2026, with the assistance of our service areas, we were able to identify 17.4 million or 2.33% efficiencies in cost savings.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: and from our operating, which are listed on the slide.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Just to highlight a few, we identified fuel savings of 1.8 million, lower cost on diesel, 1.7, then there were some increases in revenue on golf, and then IT software rationalization and contract positions, some contract positions were reduced.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: New initiatives, each year the city carefully and systematically invest in new initiatives to advance on its strategic objectives.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: New initiatives were discouraged for 2026 as part of our goal to reduce the 2026 budget impact.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Of the 14 initiatives that are proposed, 10 are either self-funded or funded through capital.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Highlights include enhancing enforcement services delivery to add coverage gaps and support proactive enforcement in high priority areas.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Adding staff in our building division to support faster housing approvals to meet city's housing objectives.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: The impact of the proposed new initiative on the 2026 budget is less than 0.3 million or 0.04% on the budget increase.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Let's talk about the capital infrastructure levy now.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: This change was extremely difficult to achieve.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: We had to rework the capital budget multiple times to balance the numbers.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Only reason to reduce this is to reduce the tax impact to our residents.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: The 2026 capital infrastructure is proposed to be calculated at 1%.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: The efforts are the following are the efforts that we did to reduce the capital infrastructure levy Citywide reduction of around 259 million or nine percent of the state of good repair over ten years deferral of major projects to future years and we did review works in progress and then were able to get a return a return of 80.5 million back to our reserves and

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So capital infrastructure levy, so first approved in 2008, helps maintain the 18.7 billion in assets that the city own.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Annual levy increases are listed on the slide here.

lines.text: Historic tax rates.

lines.text: Our priority is to always balance tax fairness with our city's need to generate enough revenue to provide the service residents rely on.

lines.text: This slide talks about the historic residential property tax rates from the past 16 years.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: 2026 proposed rate of 1.6% is below the average of 1.8% if you take the 16-year average, and it's also below the 2025 CPI of 2.2%.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: City staff has been able to mitigate the forecast budgeted increase for 2026.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: If adopted, the city's portion of the tax increase will be 1.61%.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: When combined with the region appeal, 3.6%, the total increase would be 5.21%, which is 4% less than the last year's budget increase.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: The average price of the typical home has changed, and therefore, instead of the average home, we're showing, based on the 100,000 assessed value, an increase of $16.6 for the city of Mississauga portion.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Then 37.24 is the regional appeal portion, which adds to a 53.91.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: These were all the 2026 budget-related impacts, but I would like to talk about a little bit on what's coming in the future.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: First of all, I would like to talk about the Peter Gilgan Hospital.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Looking forward into the four years forecast, Mississauga, a city of...

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Mississauga Council unanimously approved a motion from Mayor Parrish to approve a 390 million municipal contribution towards Trillium Health Partners, local share for the construction of the Peter Gilgan Hospital.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: The facility is set to become Canada's largest community-based academic hospital and is expected to reach substantial completion by 2033.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Mississauga's share to the new hospital represents a major investment in the city's future healthcare capacity.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: City of Mississauga is committed to working with its provincial and federal counterparts to ensure the needs of the Mississauga residents are met now and in the future.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: The city is proposing annual hospital levy of 1% starting 2029.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: All revenue from the levy will be directed to a dedicated reserve that will be applied directly to the city's 390 million local share contribution.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: A debenture will be issued to cover any remaining shortfalls between the approved contribution and the balance levy collected as of January 1st, 2033.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: A little bit about the GTA payments in lieu.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: In 2022, the city successfully advocated for the government of Ontario to remove the cap on the payments from the Toronto airport in lieu of taxes.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: However, the province has decided to introduce a 5% cap on the annual increases.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: This cap will take effect once the GTA reaches its pre-pandemic passenger volume, which is 49.2 million.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: As per the projections, it seems that it will be in 2029.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: If passenger volume exceeds 5% annually, Mississauga will experience a loss in potential revenue once this cap is introduced.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: The next three slides I have will talk about the long-range financial plan, which is related to item 10.5 on the agenda as well.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: The 2025 long-range financial plan strategy is an update to the original framework developed in 2016, which has guided how the city manages its finances for nearly a decade.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: During this time, it has supported disciplined decision-making and long-range financial stability.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Staff have updated the strategy to reflect current conditions, emerging pressures, and better mitigate risk with the purpose of supporting council and staff in making informed decisions that balance growth, service levels, and affordability over long-term.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: helping to achieve city the three targets outcomes that are identified here, which is to ensure sustainability, support financial flexibility, and mitigate vulnerability.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Mississauga has a strong foundation and has demonstrated adaptability to and financial strength, particularly over the past five years.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: At the same time, economic and political shifts continue to shape the municipal sector.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Pressures such as housing affordability, climate change, interest rate and inflation fluctuations continue to influence our future outlook.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: By building on the lessons learned and the financial stability established over the past decade, the city is well positioned to move forward from a place of strength and proactive planning.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: At the core of the strategy are 10 guiding principles that form the foundation for future decision-making.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: These are not new concepts, as many reflect in our existing financial policies and practices, but they have now been formalized to serve as common reference point going forward.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: The principles are designed to work together

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: as integrated and flexible framework, supporting the city as it navigates complex and often difficult decisions, particularly when balancing competing priorities and emerging pressures.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Most importantly, long-range financial plan provides clarity beyond the annual budget.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: It allows us to understand the long-range impacts of today's decisions, the trade-offs involved, and the risks of inaction.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Strengthening the city's financial resilience today and into future.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: That concludes my presentation and open to any questions.

lines.text: So I'm going to begin with one of the first of the longer questions, and that has already been addressed by Councillor Hornick.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: That is referring to the union non-union labour adjustment increase of $64.5 million.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: That's at page 12 of the budget summary.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: I certainly did not anticipate that that figure incorporated previous year labor settlements, but I realized it would include fringe benefits of approximately 30%.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: But my question is, well, several questions, actually.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: This is a multiple-quant question.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: I assume that that is based upon the number of FTEs at the end of 2026.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: And we know that figure was 5,321.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: That has come up several times in the previous deliberations.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: So I divided the total increment or salary increase of $64.5 million by the 5,321 FTEs.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: And I came up with a staggering salary increase, if I can just call it, it's more than a salary, of $12,122 per FTE.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: First of all, I wonder if Raz could corroborate that figure.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: And I'd like to know what the highest increase was received by any individual of the city staff with regards to a salary increase projected for 2026.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: I don't want to know that individual or the position, but I'd like to know the actual dollar figure.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: And I'm wondering really how these increases, if I've

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: characterize them correctly can be justified when so many taxpayers and others are struggling financially.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Okay, why don't we stop the clock for that because I know you've got a couple of questions and we'll get Faraz and or other staff to respond as much as you can and much detail as you can provide on the questions Mr. Mackey has asked.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So over to staff for that.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: Through the chair to Mr. Mackey.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: Mr. Mackey, the details of the $64.5 million

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: is because it's not just a salary increase.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: There is different components, as I was explaining to Councillor Hornick.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: There is an annualisation component of around $11 million.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: Then there's compounding of the union increases, which we're not able to get into the 2025 budget because the union settlement was done after the cut-off.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: So that's in around $26 million.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: Then we do have fringe benefits in there as well.

lines.speaker: Robert Trewartha
lines.text: Some of the unions have higher fringe benefits, like specially fire, it's around 37 to 38%.

lines.speaker: SPEAKER_09
lines.text: for non-union staff is around 28%.

lines.speaker: SPEAKER_09
lines.text: So if we reduce all of that, then the actual salary increase is not that high, but it just compounds to because of the various components to that particular item.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: So through you, Mr.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: Chair, can you actually tell Budget Committee what the average projected salary increase for 2026 will be?

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: Maybe the median increase is more reflective of the impact than the average increase, but either of those figures would be appreciated.

lines.speaker: SPEAKER_09
lines.text: Through the Chair, the average increase would be around 2.5%.

lines.speaker: SPEAKER_09
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: I'm satisfied with that.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: Those questions followed on from those from Councillor Hornick.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Okay.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Go ahead on to your next question then, Mr. Mackey.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: So I'm going to move on to the presentation on what was identified as the Peter Gilligan Hospital and the 1% levy, which will be in effect for four years, I believe, beginning in 2029.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: Do finance have a forecast of the amount of money

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: that will be raised by the levy, which will take place over four years.

lines.speaker: Councillor Brad Butt
lines.text: Through the chair.

lines.speaker: Councillor Brad Butt
lines.text: Through the chair.

lines.speaker: SPEAKER_09
lines.text: So the levy would be for five years, and then it would amount to $112 million.

lines.speaker: SPEAKER_09
lines.text: The balance, $278 million, will be issued through a debenture, and that would cover the $390 million.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: And it's that balance I wanted to ask you about.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: Has the balance of 278 million that you referred to been included in the debt forecast of approximately 100 million for 2023?

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: And that appears at page 40 of the financial forecast, which is volume one of the three-volume budget document.

lines.speaker: SPEAKER_09
lines.text: Through the chair, that particular number is not included in the projection, but we do have a forecast that we have through the levy.

lines.speaker: SPEAKER_09
lines.text: We are able to make sure that our debt levels are not going over the limit that are prescribed by the province plus our own debt limit.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: So, council, can I keep going?

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: You can do one more for sure.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Okay.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: So I want to ask you about what I perhaps unkindly considered to be the fudge factor.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: And that is referring to page 21 of the budget overview, you've included an assessment growth credit of 1.22%.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: Can you explain the basis?

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: Is that a forecast?

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: I've never seen it this high in the city's budget projections.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: And last year, for example, the credit for the assessment growth was 0.6%.

lines.speaker: SPEAKER_09
lines.text: Yes, through the chair.

lines.speaker: SPEAKER_09
lines.text: It is certainly an anomaly.

lines.speaker: SPEAKER_09
lines.text: We haven't seen this growth in the recent times.

lines.speaker: SPEAKER_09
lines.text: This is basically a transition between actual condo growth that come into assessment.

lines.speaker: SPEAKER_09
lines.text: In earlier years, the condos buildings were growing, and then when they made an assessment through MPAC,

lines.speaker: SPEAKER_09
lines.text: This is driven by the condo growth and the high rises for the 1.22%.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: This is basically a forecast?

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: No, this is actual.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: Okay, thank you.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: Thank you, Councillor Butt, for your time and attention, and thank you very much to Perez for the information he's provided.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac

lines.text: Yes, through the chair.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: We are going to do the presentation later today or possibly tomorrow.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: This is something that council has asked for the past couple of years, whether it be for parking, for parking control, compliance officers, etc.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: But certainly this will be discussed as part of the presentation.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Overall, the actual effect is going to be zero as in terms of the tax impact.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Okay, go ahead.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: And if I could just follow up in that same area, I'm curious as to why roads lost 52 FTEs in 2025.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: I thought that maintaining our roads was one of the critical infrastructure areas of the city.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Commissioner Rogers?

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Through you, Mr.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Chair.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So this 52 FTE decrease was really for two primary reasons.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: The first reason was an internal reorganization of staff.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So 34 of those 52 staff really just moved over to Andrew's department in planning and building.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: It was our former development engineering group.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So those FTE have not gone away.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: They've just been re-organized.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And the second reason are 18 FTE related to automated speed enforcement.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So these were previously approved FTEs that weren't actually filled.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: mostly related to joint processing center kind of build out.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And as council knows, that program has been turned off.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So we've turned off the FTE and given those back.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Yep.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Okay.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: And I have one final question.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: It's for another service area.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: But I'll just put it on record.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: I'm not really expecting an answer now, necessarily.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: It's for transportation and works.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: And maybe their service presentation will come up this afternoon.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: So during the past summer, that's 2025, the parking lot

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: at Huron Park, a community centre, was excavated.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: A new base was laid, followed by a smooth asphalt surface and also a brand new ramp was constructed to assist wheelchair users to get to the front door.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: What was the total cost of this project?

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: And I'm asking that you please include the gardening costs to replace the damage and destroyed shrubs and plants that took place.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: So a total cost for that project.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: Several people, because I use the Huron Park Community Center very regularly, that project went on for several months and several people remarked that they didn't understand why it was actually being undertaken at a time when the city's financial position is

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: I'm not going to say precarious, but it's in a difficult position.

lines.speaker: Councillor John Kovac
lines.text: And yes, that's my question.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: I don't expect that to be answered now, but it might be something that... No, Mr. Mackey, I think Commissioner Sheff is prepared to respond.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Yes, through the chair.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: So the Huron Park lifecycle replacement of the actual, the asphalt works and the concrete, total cost came in around 1.5 million and part of that 25,000 was going to be spent on the actual landscape works.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: This is our standard project to go and take care of our buildings and our sites and parking lots, and this was certainly going to be reviewed, and it was a necessary fix.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: And I believe the ward councillor would like to comment too.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Councillor DeMarla?

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: Thank you, Chris, for asking the question, because, you know, quite unusually, I got a fair number of questions on the parking lot looks...

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: just fine, and why has it been repaved?

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: So I want to thank the Commissioner for responding in terms of the dollar amounts, but Commissioner, maybe this is also an opportunity to clarify for my residents, because perhaps there's more to the replacement cycle than just physical aesthetics, right?

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: So a parking lot might look

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: perfectly good and Chris I you know I do appreciate the question because it's something that a number of my residents have asked I don't want you to take this the other way but I'm seeking some clarity as well in terms of being able to differentiate between aesthetically something might look just great but it might still need replacement that's what I'm guessing but Raj maybe you can educate me and my residents at the same time thank you yes to the chair

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: I am going to ask Anna to come up, who is the Director of Facilities and Property Management.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Thank you, Raj.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And through you, Mr.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Chair.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Thank you, Mr. Mackey, for your inquiry.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: It was a very good question regarding the work that we've done at Huron Park Community Center.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: This project actually is a perfect example of how we coordinated

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: a simple parking lot renewal project and looked at also addressing accessibility concerns and energy improvements.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The driver of this lifecycle project was replacing very damaged asphalt and repairing concrete sidewalks and curbs that were a hazard on site.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Approximately 150,000 square feet of asphalt was replaced in all four parking lots, as well as repairing concrete sidewalks and curbs

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: to address the uneven surfaces.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So there was a health and safety issue on the site with regards to some of these damages.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: By completing the parking lot, we had the opportunity to add an additional 13 new barrier-free parking spaces, which increases the demand in this specific facility.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So we were very proud that we were able to actually do that.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: In addition to that, we looked at our energy

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And so we took all the old light fixtures and we replaced them with new energy-efficient LED light fixtures and also installed new infrastructure for future EV charging stations.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The second part of the scope, which was another major focus for us, was the accessibility features.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: And not only did we improve on some, we added additional accessibility features.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So building the new ramp,

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: at the main entrance to comply with our accessibility design standards was a very important feature as part of this project.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: It actually reduced the distance people had to travel by 55 feet, which is significant.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: In order to facilitate this first placement, we had to remove the old ramp, we had to remove the existing vegetation, construct a new retaining wall, a new ramp, and as well as put in new landscaping area.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: When the planning of the landscaped area was done, we worked closely with parks so that we can identify the appropriate vegetation for this area.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: As Raj had indicated, the construction cost for all of this work was about 1.4 million, and the vegetation or the landscaping was about $25,000.

lines.speaker: Chris Mackie
lines.text: Okay, thank you very much.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: And through the chair, honorable mayor, members of council, colleagues, and members of the public,

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Thank you for the opportunity to speak today.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: I'll be providing highlights of our 2026 business plan and proposed budget.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: We care for and protect spaces that make Mississauga livable.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: We protect what matters, safeguarding our natural environment, create spaces to thrive, vibrant parks and public places for everyone, and we lead with purpose, raising the bar for outdoor experiences and stewardship.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: This plan is about delivering our mandate and creating greener, safer, more resilient Mississauga.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Our work touches every resident, every day.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Our assets tell a story of connection, recreation, and sustainability.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: We maintain hundreds of parks, playgrounds, and kilometers of trails, and thousands of hectares of natural areas.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Sports and recreation thrive with fields and facilities for everything from soccer to baseball and cricket.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: and we're building greener futures with hundreds of thousands of trees planted while diverting millions of kilograms of waste from landfills.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Every park, trail, and tree is an investment in community well-being.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Our focus is on creating new spaces and upgrading our existing ones to meet the demands of a growing city.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: We will design and develop new parks and major developments, continue strategic parkland acquisitions to support areas of new housing and areas of parkland deficit,

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: and redevelop various parks and amenities as part of lifecycle renewals.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Oops, sorry.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: We will continue to deliver on our future directions plan and continue to expand our urban forest canopy.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Updating and implementing the climate change action plans keeps us on track for our sustainability goals.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: These objectives aren't just projects, but they're commitments to growth, sustainability, and community.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Our operating budget is about maintaining our core services.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: 45 million represents 6% of the city's budget, primarily allocated towards maintaining our parks and forestry assets.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: The net 1.1 million increase is primarily due to labor and benefit adjustments.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Offsets and efficiencies are primarily due to growth deferrals and renewal.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: There are no new requests this year for new initiatives.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: The 2026 capital budget is 90 million or 19% of the net capital budget.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: With reduced DC and CIL revenues, we must balance preserving what we have while building for the future.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: In 2026, we're investing 90.5 million in key projects.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Highlights include 45 million for parkland acquisitions to secure future green space,

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: 3.9 million in sport field and court rehabilitation, 3.7 million in washroom upgrades, 2.6 million in trail reconstruction, 2.5 million in bridge rehabilitation, and 1.2 million in playground redevelopments.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: These investments keep our park safe, modern, and ready for growth.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Parkland acquisition represents approximately 50% of the budget, followed by redevelopments at approximately 30% and new developments at approximately 14%.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Our investment over the next 10 years include the Lakeview Village Park redevelopment, which will add new parks, including a landmark cultural pier, an extension of the waterfront trail,

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: The Cooksville Parkland Development, which will create a 50-acre park in Cooksville that will integrate signature components, enhanced recreation, and flood resiliency.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: The Arendelle Trail Rehabilitation and Realignment in partnership with Transportation and Works, which will improve shoreline protection and provide various trail improvements to minimize flooding impacts.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: The Douglas Kennedy All-Access Artificial Turf Baseball Diamond in partnership with the Region of Peel,

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: which will feature our first fully accessible artificial turf diamond built above the Region of Peel's water reservoir facility, and the design and construction of the former Grand Highlands golf course.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: We will be investing $700 million over the next 10 years.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Our budget and business plan reflect our ongoing operations and investment in what residents value in our parks and natural areas.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: And with that, I leave you with this image of the newly developed Credit Meadows Trail.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: I'd like to thank the entire Parks, Forestry and Environment team, led by the leadership team behind me, for their contributions and commitment to serving this community.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: I'm going to be presenting the library's 26th budget proposal and our four-year business plan.
