lines.text: Go ahead, Diana.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: On the right-hand side of your screen, you'll see the organizational structure of the library, and as per the Provincial Public Libraries Act, we have a governing library board.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Operationally, the library consists of four major areas of specialization.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: On the left-hand side,

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: you'll see the library's new mission, vision, and value statements.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Those were developed last year by the library board as per the future directions library plan to better reflect how the library aims to serve the community.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Next slide, please.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: The library, as I said, operates under the mandate of the Public Libraries Act.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: In alignment with this Act and the Municipal Act, Council appoints members to the Mississauga Public Library Board 60 days after a municipal election.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: At a higher level, the Public Libraries Act sets out the framework for library service.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: In addition to the Public Libraries Act, the library follows other legislative requirements, such as the Employment Standards Act, the Occupational Health and Safety Act, the Municipal Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act,

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: or MFIPA, just to name a few.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Next slide, please.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Wanted to share some of our key performance indicators for 2024.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: All the KPIs that you see on the slide are up from previous years.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: In 2024, almost 7 million items were borrowed by library customers, so lots of folks still reading.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Library programs continue to be of great interest, with many people attending a wide range of programs.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: The number of people, customers rather, up in the open window hub was up to 6,700, which was up

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: more than 2,000 from the previous year.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: One KPI that's not on the screen that I want to share is our foot traffic, which exceeded 3.7 million.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: I think in all the metrics speak to the fact that the library is well used by members of the community every day.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Next slide.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Also wanted to share some of our accomplishments.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: In the interest of time, I will just touch on a few.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We expanded the offerings of our Library of Things collection and added more story walks to municipal parks.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Our popular TD Summer Reading Club was well attended, offering a variety of programs in helping children avoid the summer reading slump when they return to school in the fall.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Lastly, I'll note that as a result of streamlined administrative opening procedures,

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: we were also able to introduce an additional 30 minutes of service in the morning, Monday through Thursday, at no additional cost across 14 locations.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Next slide, please.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: The key objectives for the business plan in the period include completing the redevelopment of the South Common Library

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: to meet the changing needs of the community, expanding the Makerspace initiative to support creativity and innovation, and increasing community access to library resources.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: This includes our laptop kiosk expansion, growing our collection, both physical and digital, and implementation of express libraries.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Next slide.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: The Mississauga Library budget accounts for 4% of the city's net operating budget.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: For 2026, the overall change from the previous year is proposed to be a net increase of $2.4 million.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Next slide.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: This $2.4 million increase, or 8.1%, is the amount required to maintain current service levels.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: The vast majority of this increase is related to labour and benefits adjustments associated with the requirements of the collective agreement.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Next slide, please.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: The library has one new initiative requiring a budget request in 2026, which is essentially self-funded.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Now that the Hazel McCallion Central Library has been open for a couple of years, we have identified a greater need for more supervisors to support operations and better serve our customers.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: To achieve this, we are proposing taking four permanent part-time page positions and converting them to a full-time supervisory position.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Next slide.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: The Mississauga Library's 2026 capital budget proposes an increase of $1.4 million over 2025 and overall would account for 1% of the city's net capital budget for 2026.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Next slide.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So the details

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: for the $8.29 million capital budget for 2026 is primarily for the ongoing South Common Library renovation.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: The budget also includes physical library material, design work for two major facility projects with the goal of being shovel-ready for future funding opportunities from other levels of government,

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: It also includes the development of a makerspace at the Aaron Meadows Library since we're already going to be doing work at that facility and we want to take advantage of the timing and avoid a second service disruption for customers in the future.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Smart people counters to replace malfunctioning equipment so we can continue to meet provincial reporting obligations for grant funding and have accurate data to report on KPIs and the expansion of our popular laptop kiosk programs.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Next slide.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: The library's 10-year capital forecast is largely built from recommendations in the 2024 Future Directions Library Plan.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: It focuses on investing in our infrastructure, again, completing the South Common Library renovation, two other major facility library projects that includes design, construction, equipment, and technology, physical library material, which is a core aspect of library service,

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And finally, the capital budget also includes library-specific technology and furniture and equipment, life cycle replacement, so we ensure a state of good repair.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Next slide.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Thank you for your attention and your consideration, and I'm happy to take your questions.

lines.text: So let's move on to our next service presentation, 10.1.3, Recreation and Culture.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So good morning, Chair and members of Budget Committee.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: I'm very pleased to be here to present my first budget presentation for Recreation and Culture.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So Recreation and Culture works to keep Mississauga residents healthy, active, and connected by delivering a wide range of programs, services, and events that reflect the community's needs and celebrates its diverse cultural experiences.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Our service areas are organized into these five sections that you see here on the slide.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Both full-time and part-time staff, we have a great full-time and part-time staff team who deliver these programs who are very skilled, experienced, and community-focused.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: They plan and deliver programs, manage facilities, ensure services are safe, inclusive, and accessible for all of our residents.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: In 2025, Recreation and Culture in Mississauga achieves significant successes that strengthen our community's health connectedness and connected to our vision around more people more often in partnership with the community.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We increased participation among older adults through our free 65 plus membership.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We improved the registration process through sessional registration sessions.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We increased partnerships with hospitals to support the therapeutic needs of our community.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: While these are only a few examples, the work that is aligned that we have shared aligns with future direction plans for recreation and culture, youth and older adult plans,

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: our various cultural strategies, and the equity, diversity, and inclusion strategy as well.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: On this slide, you'll get an example of our service overview.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We are privileged to have a wide variety of infrastructure to support the community's needs.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: In the fall of 2025, we are also privileged to have the museum portfolio transferred over into recreation and culture.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: This move complements the programming and services provided by both areas, allowing us to better align our offerings, enhance opportunities for residents.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Looking at the numbers and the impact in the service areas, it is quite significant.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Our community centers and arenas welcomed over 12 million visitors.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: There were over 100,000 rounds of golf and over 600 events that were hosted in cultural facilities.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And our museums, new to our portfolio, also engaged and welcomed over 20,000 participants and visitors.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: While this is only a snapshot of the work done by our teams, these figures clearly illustrate the depth and the breadth of the engagement our services provide across the city.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Our key objectives over the next planning period include renewal and optimization of infrastructure, and you've heard a little bit about that through previous presentations, but we continue to invest in our amenities through lifecycle replacement, increase access for youth,

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: through programs, services and spaces, through the hub model development in collaboration with young people.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So a very similar model to the Moulton Youth Hub, where we're engaging youth at a very early stage to be involved in planning the new hub facility.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And supporting diverse communities by offering programs, services and events that reflect their needs.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Our events team works collaboratively with the community to foster a number of cultural events in the downtown and in various other destinations.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Recreation and Cultures budget accounts for 4% of the city's overall net operating budget.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: For 2026, the overall change for the 2025 budget proposes a net increase of just under $500,000.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: The impact in maintaining current service levels is a net increase of 452,923, or 1.4%.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: The primary cost drivers is maintaining current service levels, totaling 2.4.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: These pressures are a result of an increase in labor and benefits, balanced by a slight decrease in utilities, as well as a 500,000 in transfers from that reserve for grants.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: The combination of these items support our service areas in delivering existing programs and at our current standards.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: To address these pressures, staff have also identified 2.2 in efficiency and cost saving opportunities, significantly reducing the overall impact.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: In addition, there is a request for $276,000 proposed to be allocated to a new initiative that I'll address in just a moment.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Overall, strong efficiency measures offset the majority of the cost increase by over 90%, allowing us to maintain services, advance key priorities and limit the net increase to 1.4%.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Our new initiative that is proposed for 2026 is for our recreation support staff, inclusion services and program supports.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Mississauga has an opportunity to strengthen its leadership in inclusive recreation by introducing a free year-round one-to-one inclusion support services for participants with disabilities across registered recreation and culture programs.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: This service, which is already offered in surrounding municipalities, would ensure consistent and equitable access for Mississauga residents who require additional support to participate fully.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: To deliver this program, the request includes one permanent FTE and budget for temporary staff as well.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Implementation will be phased for the program being fully operational by Q3 of 2027, enabling us to sustain growth, ensure staff training and service quality.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Shifting gears to Recreation and Culture's 2026 capital budget proposes a net decrease of $6.1 million, reflecting the deliberate approach to phasing and deferring projects to future years to ensure alignment with timing and capacity and readiness, overall accounting for 9% of the city's net capital budget.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: 2026 capital projects are proposed to be funded at 51.8 million these investments focus on key assets including additions and renovation of the lakeview golf course maintenance building and the design phase for several multi-use community facilities some of which rona referenced in her presentation but mississauga valley community center and library cooksville community center and library

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: and the small arms inspection building, positioning these projects for future delivery.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: The development, as you know, of South Common Community Center and library is underway, and the Glen Forest Youth Hub redevelopment development also reinforces the city's commitment to youth focused spaces and programming.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And that concludes my presentation.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish

lines.text: River Grove Community Center as well as Meadowville Community Center are our busiest locations when it comes to that type of programming.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Good morning, Madam Mayor and members of the committee.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: I'm pleased to present the 2026-2029 Business Plan for Facilities and Property Management Division.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: I'll walk you through our current service levels, our operating and capital budgets, and key priorities.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: I will also highlight some major projects that support other service areas across the city.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Facilities and property management consists of five sections, which are responsible for facility planning, managing capital construction projects, and maintaining facilities across the city.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: We're also responsible for managing utilities and energy programs, as well as operating city hall and other corporate administrative buildings.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Our team of 125 professionals are focused on delivering several key projects, services, and objectives over the next four years.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Some of the key objectives include optimizing our asset management plan by identifying investments for lifecycle improvements, delivering citywide capital projects that modernize infrastructure and set the benchmark for municipal facilities,

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: maintaining operational excellence at Civic Precinct, including City Hall, and responding promptly to service requests and emergencies, allowing for reliable operations across all facilities.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: We also implement the city's multi-year accessibility plan and invents the city's climate action goals through various energy and utility initiatives such as BES upgrades and solar PV installations.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Our goal is simple, to deliver safe, energy efficient and inclusive spaces that support the city's vision and the community needs.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Our service area manages a diverse portfolio of over 350 facilities, totaling approximately 5.6 million square feet of building area valued at $2.9 billion.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: With over 65% of our facilities more than 30 years old, we manage several thousand service requests annually to address preventative and demand maintenance.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: To help offset these pressures, facilities and property management will oversee approximately $50 million in capital lifecycle renewals in 2026.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: These investments are critical to ensure we maintain our buildings in a state of good repair.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Our service mandate is driven by several corporate strategies to plan, prioritize capital investments that deliver on council priorities

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: and provide value for money.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: For 2026, our net operating budget increases by $1.2 million.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Key drivers to this increase include labor and benefits, higher preventative and demand and emergency maintenance costs due to aging buildings, project management software licensing, the fixed service delivery charge for water,

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The FPM operating budget represents 3% of the city's overall operating budget.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: To help offset these pressures, Facilities and Property Management has identified $110,000 in efficiencies with no impact on service levels.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Most of these efficiencies come from a further review of our processes and tools.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: In 2026, we forecast

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: a 4% decrease in city-wide utility budget, which is mainly attributed to the decrease in hydro and gas consumption, which continues to decline through optimizing equipment and implementing decarbonization projects.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: This decreased trend, however, is offset by an increasing water cost and the new Region Appeals fixed service delivery charge, which is based on the meter size.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: This next chart demonstrates that while utility rates continue to rise, the city remains committed to mitigating these impacts through decarbonization and energy conservation initiatives.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: In 2026, our capital budget is approximately $50 million, which allows us to maintain our buildings in a state of good repair.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The facility and property management share of the city's capital budget is approximately 11%.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: In 2025, I'm very excited to share that we completed several important capital projects to support accessibility, heritage preservation, and advance our energy mandates.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: We also completed several projects to enhance program spaces in our community facilities and renovated critical infrastructure to support emergency services to ensure business continuity.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: This slide highlights several other achievements delivered over the past year.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: These projects demonstrate the range of facility and property management's work across recreation, transit, corporate facilities, and this is just to name a few.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: We also completed the updates to our facility accessibility design standards, which supports our commitment to removing barriers in our city buildings and on our sites.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Looking ahead in 2026, we have several exciting, significant capital projects advancing across the city.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Some key projects include the South Common Community Center and Library, the new Fire Station 128, the design for both the Mississauga Valley's Community Center and Library, and the new Cooksville Community Center and Library to make it shovel-ready.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: In addition, we will begin designing for projection mapping on the city hall facade, which will further animate the celebration square.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The 10-year capital plan includes approximately $485 million worth of lifecycle renewal.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: In addition, facilities and property management will continue to support

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: the implementation of other division capital plans through the design and execution of building construction projects.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Thank you very much for listening, and I'd be happy to take any questions.

lines.text: Thank you very much, Anna.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And our final presentation, and then we'll talk about a couple of the reports if we want to pass those today.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: But we'll start with regulatory services.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So welcome, Georgios.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Over to you.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Good afternoon, Chair and members of Budget Committee.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: I am pleased to present a service area overview of regulatory services

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: along with a phase two budget request focusing on strengthening frontline enforcement, modernizing service delivery, and ensuring the division is prepared to meet the city's evolving needs.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: As council will recall, last year I outlined a phase two year approach to this budget request designed to support responsible resource allocation, operational scaling, and sustainable service improvements

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Council approved the 2025 portion of that plan, and I'm here today to present phase two, which completes that approach.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: With that foundation now in place, we are positioned to move forward into the next phase, focus again on modernization, enhance visibility, and strengthen capacity and responsiveness across the city.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Building on that foundation, I'll begin with an overview of regulatory services mandate and how the division is structured to deliver on it.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Our mandate is to maintain order, safety, and community standards by ensuring compliance with the city's bylaws through a comprehensive and balanced approach that integrates awareness, education, and enforcement.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: As Mississauga continues to grow and become more complex, our mandate has expanded and evolved, requiring modern enforcement tools, updated regulatory frameworks, and increasingly integrated service delivery.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Regulatory services now spans nine core service areas

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: reflecting the breadth and complexity of the division's responsibilities.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Together, the division administers and enforces more than 40 bylaws and delivers services that directly affect residents and businesses every day.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: As one of Canada's largest municipalities, Mississauga requires an enforcement framework that operates at scale and with sophistication, supporting broader municipal objectives such as prevention, accountability, and community well-being.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Together, this structure positions the city to meet today's regulatory needs while building the capacity to support future growth, modernization, and council priorities.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Having outlined regulatory services mandate and organizational structure, it is important to highlight the scope and operational reality of that mandate.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: It translates into a wide-ranging, high-volume service environment that requires both strong frontline service delivery and strategic coordinated enforcement approaches.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: The division now responds to tens of thousands of service requests each year across a wide range of regulatory and enforcement areas.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Our work spans everything from licensing taxis and ride-sharing platforms, and inspecting short-term rentals and business premises, to parking enforcement, corporate security services at city parks and facilities, and transit enforcement.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Our services address some of the most visible day-to-day community issues, including nuisance behavior, noise enforcement, and tenant protections through the Mississauga Rental Standards Program.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Specialized housing enforcement and property standards teams are increasingly critical as the housing landscape evolves and demand grows for safe, well-maintained living conditions.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: We also deliver essential community services, including animal services, encompassing wildlife response, pet adoption and licensing, shelter operations,

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: and the continued expansion of animal care and public programs.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: As expectations continue to rise, so does the need for a proactive, data-informed enforcement model that anticipates emerging issues, strengthens expertise, and ensures the city is positioned to respond effectively and responsibly.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: To bring that mandate to life, it is important to look at what service delivery looks like on the ground.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: These figures illustrate the scale and real-world impact of regulatory services and the growing demand for municipal enforcement and regulatory programs.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Last year, the Division responded to more than 80,000 requests for investigation, reflecting the size of Mississauga and the increasing volume and complexity of community concerns.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Licensing, inspection, and compliance remain core functions, including over 10,000 vehicle-for-hire inspections, more than 6,100 business licenses, and 4,600 mobile business licenses issued, and the registration of 355 apartment buildings to strengthen housing oversight and tenant protections.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Our work also delivers direct community and public well-being outcomes, including 422

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: cat and 144 dog adoptions through animal services and 254 charity gaming licenses that support organizations serving vulnerable populations The division also supports critical corporate safety and risk mitigation functions responding to more than 7,400 service requests conducting over 3,400 proactive security investigations and completing more than 2,400 video surveillance review requests across city facilities and public spaces

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: While these figures illustrate demand, they only tell part of the story.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: They do not fully capture the growing complexity of enforcement, the investigative depth now required in many of the cases we oversee, and the increased need for proactive, coordinated operations.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Together, these figures reinforce that regulatory services operates in a high-volume, increasingly complex service environment, underscoring the importance of a coordinated, modern, data-informed enforcement model to support council priorities

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: community safety, and the city's continued growth.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Given the scale, volume, and growing complexity of our service environment, the division must continue to evolve.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: As Mississauga grows and community needs and expectations change, our objectives for the 2026 to 2029 years focus on strengthening service delivery, modernizing enforcement, and supporting safe, resilient neighborhoods.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Essential priority is enhancing bylaw enforcement service delivery, including operational coverage and responsiveness, addressing service gaps, reducing response time, and strengthening enforcement in high priority and increasingly complex operational areas.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: We are focused on strengthening operational capacity and effectiveness.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Our key priorities include strengthening public education and prevention, increasing enforcement visibility and collaboration with community partners,

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: and advancing a future-ready animal services model focused on enhanced capacity, improved animal care outcomes, reduced shelter stays, and again, data-driven service enhancements.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Together, these priorities form a multi-year roadmap to ensure the division has the capacity and capability to meet today's demands and respond effectively to the challenges of a growing, more complex city.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: As each of those objectives relies on staffing and operational investments, I will now turn to the Division's 2026 budget request.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: To advance the objectives just outlined, the Division's 2026 budget request represents phase two of a two-year approach that Council approved last year.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: It builds directly on the 2025 investments that stabilize service levels and strengthen frontline capacity.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: The 2026 request includes 58 FTEs and associated costs that are fully offset by revenues, enabling the city to strengthen enforcement services in a fiscally responsible way without impacting the tax levy.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: This request is not about growth for growth's sake.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: It is about closing service gaps

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: improving how we deliver today's services, and building the foundation for a future-ready enforcement system.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: The requested resources are targeted where operational pressure, risk, and community expectations are highest.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Across animal services, parking services, and compliance licensing and enforcement, the requested FTEs are focused on one core function, strengthening frontline capacity in high-demand, high-impact service areas.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Turning to the overall operating picture, the proposed 2026 net operating budget for regulatory services is $16.6 million, representing a $2.1 million increase.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: At approximately 2% of the city's net operating budget, regulatory services delivers a strong return on investment.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Maintaining current service levels continues to be driven primarily by labor and benefit pressures, which accounts for approximately $5.9 million of the increase.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Those pressures are partially offset through increased revenues, the division delivering on some efficiencies, and also to a net budget decrease as a result of the new Enhanced Enforcement Service Delivery Initiative.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: As outlined in phase two of the budget request, the Enhanced Enforcement Service Delivery Initiative reflects an investment required for 58 FTEs to strengthen frontline enforcement capacity with all operating costs

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: fully offset through revenues.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And with that, the net capital budget increase to support the division in 2026 totals 4.8 million.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Thank you for the opportunity to present to Budget Committee.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Before I do conclude, I'd like to note for Council and the public that the subject that is shown in the photo here was found ultimately to be non-compliant, but was very cooperative.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Education was provided, and the matter was ultimately resolved with a warning.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So, a great photo op here for one of our residents at the animal shelter.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: I'm available to answer any questions you might have.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Well, I certainly know that's not my dog.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Anyway, questions for you, Georgios?

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Councillor Hornet.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: I showed my four-and-a-half-year-old that picture.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: He said, oh, that's silly.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: I like it.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: I've got quite a series of questions, so forgive me for maybe holding everybody from lunch.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Starting off, just to acknowledge, we probably ask your department to produce the most number of reports of any department.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: You've been a very hot commodity and topic the last little while.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: And I'd also note, just to give credit to the public survey we had, you were in the part where you have a high importance from the public.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: but they have a low satisfaction.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: And that would indicate that would be a place you'd want to invest.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Hence your ask.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: But I have questions about your ask.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: So on the ask itself, you have 58 new staff, and you show it to be revenue neutral on the budget request.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: But the department itself has not been revenue neutral in the last while.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: And I guess I'd like to further understand that.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: So over the last two years, the statistics given, revenues risen by 34% over two years, $7 million.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Expenses have gone up 45.5% or 13 and a half million.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: So the gap is 6.7 million or 11%.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: So I guess I just want to understand how we have that gap that's grown over the last two years, and it's a growing gap, versus the budget request in front of us, which is revenue neutral.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: What's the difference there between the two things?

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Sure, through the chair.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Most of our revenue, sorry, the offset that occurs to revenue primarily comes from parking enforcement, so the more officers we have out there that are able to ultimately issue tickets, that would ultimately increase our revenue.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Until last year, parking enforcement hadn't seen a large influx in their FTE complement, so as we now start scaling up that FTE base in parking enforcement, we will see

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: greater revenues coming in through that stream.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Additionally, over the years, we haven't been issuing, or better yet, our re-inspection fees have been a little bit lower, but as council approved through fees and charges this year, we have a phased approach to re-inspection fees, the first fee being $400, the second one being $800, the third fee being $1,200.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So we're now going to start seeing us being able to recoup some of the costs associated with conducting our day-to-day enforcement activities.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Okay.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: And your stats there for response times and stuff are very good.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: They're all in the 90s, with the exception, as you just pointed out, parking enforcement, 87.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: A very high percentage still, right?

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: I'll give you that.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: The two that come to mind, though, here is the shelter save rate of cats and dogs.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: You're estimating in this current, or last current year, 95%.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: But you're expecting that to go down by 5%.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Why would that be if we're adding more staff?

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So unfortunately, it's the type of animals that come into the animal shelter.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Sometimes they do have significant behavioral issues, right?

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: And it's not always possible to rehabilitate an animal.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: What I'd like to see us do more of is provide more opportunities to do that, but there's a cost associated with that, which is why, as part of our FT request for animal services, we're looking at how we can better implement some

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: training protocols and behavioral protocols to better rehabilitate those animals.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So we'll do our best to ensure that an animal can be adopted out, but sometimes it's just not feasible given maybe the environment that we rescued them from.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Yeah, I appreciate that.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: I mean, and your staff have been really good taking me out for a tour quite a while ago and educating me on those challenges of individual animals and trying to recuperate them.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: I just don't understand that why would it drop when we're adding more resources?

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So the resources aren't the direct impact.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: We're just looking at the trends that are currently occurring out in terms of the animals we're bringing in.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So the resources doesn't necessarily mean we're going to be able to say

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: an animal that came from a kind of a horrendous situation where there was abuse and neglect.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We're just always trying to be very transparent with our save rate.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: That being said,

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Even 95% is extremely high for a save rate.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Listen, I want it to be 100% if we can get it there.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: But again, some of the animals we've been taking in as of late, some more challenges, right?

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: A lot of pit bull mixes in animals where they're coming from dog fighting operations, those types of things.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So those are really challenging animals to rehabilitate.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Where we can, we do our best.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Well, thank you for that work because it is deeply heart-wrenching when you see those cases.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: The last one I'll just pull on is on-site citywide security responses.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Again, we have a 99% response rate this year.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: You're expecting that to decline to 95.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Why would it be a decline when we're hiring more staff?

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Sure, through the chair.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: What we're doing right now is, if council will recall, in September of 2025, security services joined the enforcement division.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So over the last year and a bit, I've been, well, it's been less than a year, but since September of 2025, I've really been taking a look at number of service calls, where there's opportunities for improvement.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: You know, we're now...

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: um you know it's been a few months um sorry it was september of 2024 we've had a full year better yet under our belt and we're looking at ways to further improve our response times that being said our service calls continue to grow in security services and there hasn't been a budget request for an increase in staffing complement so what i'd like to do is really look at how we can be more efficient more effective with existing staffing complement we have and then if required i'll come back to council to determine

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Do we need more resources to respond to our ever-growing city, more city facilities, and the calls for service ultimately that we're receiving?

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Thank you again.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: As I said, we started by saying we've put a lot on your staff, and every single one of the areas you take is a place that we have a lot of activity, a lot more demands from the public, a lot more demands from council.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: So thank you very much for you and your staff's work.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Andrew Grantham
lines.text: Councillor Hornick, Mayor Parrish.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: Well, I'll start off by saying

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: It was a very lucky day for our city that you came to work for us.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: I think you do an incredible job.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: And to look at doing more with less is admirable.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: Not many of us do that.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: I don't even do it.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: I'm a little curious, though.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: We've done a lot of changes in animal services, and one of the things we do do is we keep animals longer than the three days before you put them down if they're not adopted.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: Are we still doing those changes?

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: put downs just locally, or are we getting veterinarians in to do them?

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Through the chair, we always get a veterinarian to do it, so it's not done in-house.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We consult with veterinarians.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We ensure that there's no other opportunities to save that animal before we make that really tough decision.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: I'm glad to hear that because I know back when I was first on council, there was these nasty little boxes, and they put carbon monoxide into them, and I was just freaking.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: So how we treat our animals is pretty much how we treat our people.

lines.speaker: Councillor Alvin Tedjo
lines.text: And I'm really glad to hear that that's permanent now, and thank you very much.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Thank you, Madam Mayor.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Councillor Hart.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Thank you, and through you, the Chair.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Georges, I think you are probably the staff person that I talk to most in the entire building, probably some weeks more often than I do my own staff, it feels like.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: I am really excited...

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: to see that you're looking at a comms resource, mostly because I don't know that you should always be the face and voice of some of these things.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: But I'm wondering if you can expand a little bit in terms of how that can help you with the public education part and the other issues, because I do feel like, you know, when people talk about satisfaction, sometimes it is understanding of what the bylaws are and other things.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: So can we add a little, like, flesh that part out?

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: It's like this last little line.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: you know, after everything else.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Absolutely.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Through the chair, that resource actually was specifically as a result of the ask for the fireworks enforcement.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So if you recall, council did approve $300,000.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: And we wanted to make sure that in order to effectively and efficiently use those resources, we had a strategic communications FT that can be, you know, used that can really help

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: ultimately deliver the message to the public.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So that's what that's specifically in regards to.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: That being said, we still do engage a lot with our colleagues in strategic communications, and we do have a resource internally that does community engagement, where we really about proactive education, because I really do feel that that is the best way to tackle enforcement issues, is if you educate, down the line you can see some cost savings in terms of resourcing and FTE.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So it's top of mind for the division.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We need to do more proactive education.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: and certainly this resource will help particularly on the fireworks front, but we do have a resource from the BR approved last year that's going to allow us to do a lot more community engagement on the rental standards program, property standards, short-term accommodations, so a lot of good, fun, exciting work coming down the pipeline on that front.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: And through the chair, one more question.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: When we're talking about the complex issues, you've mentioned nuisance gatherings, other sorts of things.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: In the other report, you talk about how it may be linked to housing in brackets.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: I'm just wondering if you can expand on where the housing in brackets is.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Is that purely on the short-term rentals?

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: What's that look like?

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Through the chair, it's on everything.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: Certainly STAs and the rental standards program is where, for example, our specialized housing enforcement team focuses a lot of their time and efforts.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: But on the property standards front, and I can use Ward 5 as a great example, we see that the pilot works, right?

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We see if you increase enforcement, you have more visibility on the ground, you follow through with regular touch points with the community, you can see some really good outcomes when it comes to compliance on the property standards front, right?

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: So

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We see it across the board in all the sections, even on the parking front.

lines.speaker: Chair Carolyn Parrish
lines.text: We see a lot of positive outcomes when it comes to increasing enforcement in communities and neighborhoods.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: Thank you very much.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: I just wanted to flush those parts out a little bit, especially for the public.

lines.speaker: Councillor Sue McFadden
lines.text: These are long committee meetings, they're long sessions, but your group does some incredibly important work in our communities.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Thank you very much, Councillor Hart.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: I don't think I see anyone else on the board.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Councillor Hart, would you move?

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Receipt of the deputation.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Thank you very much.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: All in favour?

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: That is carried.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Now, there are 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 corporate reports, 10.2 through 10.6.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: We can deal with them today if you think it's going to be quick, or we can defer them and have them on the agenda for tomorrow.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: What would you like to do?

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Tomorrow?

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Okay.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: So no other business.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: We don't have a closed session.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Is there anything else anyone wanted to raise today?

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: If not, I'll entertain a motion from Councillor Fonseca to adjourn.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: All in favour?

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Christina Kakaflikas
lines.text: We'll see you tomorrow morning.
